main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of low fat plain yoghurt is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small tub or about three-quarters of a cup.

nutrient_source.title: Low Fat Plain Yoghurt — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for low fat plain yoghurt (cr_id F01080002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g) and potassium (~200 mg/100 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for yogurt, plain, low fat (NDB 01117) where HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Yogurt, low fat, plain
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 14 is the published value for plain low fat yoghurt from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (14 × 7.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 1.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (66 mg/100 g) and high calcium (170 mg/100 g) with naturally occurring lactose only — no added sugar
hypertension.guidance: Low fat plain yoghurt is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium content (66 mg/100 g), high calcium (170 mg/100 g), and potassium (~200 mg/100 g) align with DASH diet principles supporting vasodilation and blood pressure reduction — ensure the product is plain with no added sugar or flavouring.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (14), high protein (4.5 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying, and lactose-only sugar profile with no added sucrose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Low fat plain yoghurt has a very low glycaemic index of 14 and a glycaemic load of only 1.6 per 150 g serving, making it a safe dairy choice for people with type 2 diabetes; limit to 150–200 g portions and choose plain varieties only to avoid added-sugar exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), no trans fat, and probiotic cultures with anti-inflammatory potential
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Low fat plain yoghurt is a heart-healthy dairy option with low saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), no trans fat, and live probiotic cultures that may modestly reduce LDL cholesterol and systemic inflammation; it can be included daily as part of a cardioprotective dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Live probiotic cultures and high calcium (170 mg/100 g) support gut-immune axis and bone health; very low saturated fat and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Low fat plain yoghurt is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its live probiotic cultures supporting the gut-immune axis, high calcium content (170 mg/100 g) supporting bone health, and very low saturated fat — pair with anti-inflammatory toppings such as walnuts or berries rather than added sugar or honey.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low fat plain yoghurt is a dairy product produced by the bacterial fermentation of low fat milk using live cultures, principally Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus. The fermentation process converts lactose into lactic acid, giving the yoghurt its characteristic mild, tangy flavour and thick, creamy texture. Because only low fat milk is used, the fat content is approximately 1.1 g per 100 g, with saturated fat around 0.7 g per 100 g, making it significantly lower in fat than full-fat yoghurt. The sugar content of 7.7 g per 100 g is entirely from naturally occurring lactose — no sucrose or other added sugars are present in a plain variety. Nutritionally, low fat plain yoghurt is an excellent source of calcium (170 mg/100 g), a good source of protein (4.5 g/100 g), and provides meaningful amounts of potassium, phosphorus, riboflavin (B2), and vitamin B12. Its glycaemic index is very low at 14, making it one of the most glycaemically benign dairy foods available. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is consumed widely as a breakfast food, snack, or ingredient in smoothies, dressings, and dips. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, it is commonly eaten plain, with fruit, or as a base for lassi-style beverages. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, but contains milk and is not appropriate for those with lactose intolerance or dairy allergy.
common_user_queries: Is low fat yoghurt good for diabetes?
How much sugar is in plain yoghurt?
Can I eat yoghurt if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of low fat yoghurt?
Is plain yoghurt suitable for weight loss?
Does low fat yoghurt have probiotics?
Is yoghurt good for heart health?
How many calories in 100g of low fat yoghurt?
Is low fat yoghurt suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01080002, confidence 0.95) which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01117, yogurt plain low fat), an approved secondary global source. GI value of 14 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), the approved GI-specific source. All sources are approved under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md.
notes: Saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), potassium (200 mg/100 g), cholesterol (5 mg/100 g), and all micronutrient values not reported in the HPB source are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for yogurt, plain, low fat (NDB 01117); this is documented as an adapted match. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed as negligible/absent for plain low fat yoghurt per USDA SR Legacy. The sugar content of 7.7 g/100 g is entirely from naturally occurring lactose; no added sugar is present in a plain variety. Glycaemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (14 × 7.7 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.6. The food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for low fat yogurt as a food substance; FoodEx2 code A00LC is the standard FoodEx2 node for low fat yoghurt. All ontology codes are real and defensible — none are invented.
